Facts.text: St Fabian ends his reign as Catholic Pope (236-50)
Giovanni di Sabina elected Pope Sylvester III
According to legend, freeholder Lalli slays English crusader Bishop Henry with an axe on the ice of Lake Köyliönjärvi in Finland
First English Parliament summoned other than by royal command, in this instance by Simon de Montfort, Earl of Leicester. Meets in Westminster Hall.
Duke Wladyslaw Lokietek becomes King of Poland
Edward Balliol abdicates as King of Scots
The present-day location of Rio de Janeiro is first explored
Casa de Contratación (House of Trade) established by the Crown of Castile in the port of Seville to deal with American affairs
Christian IIis forced to abdicate as King of Denmark and Norway
The Mexican city of León is founded by order of viceroy Don Martín Enríquez de Almansa
Peace of Knarod ends War of Kalmar between Denmark & Sweden
Convention of Goa signed between Portugal and England,  ending their ongoing conflict in Asia and opening Portuguese ports, including China, to English trade
Cornerstone of Amsterdam townhall laid
Treaty of Andrussovo ends 13 year war between Poland & Russia
1st American military court martial trial begins in Cambridge, Massachusetts
1st edition of Pieter It Hoens "Post of Neder-Rhijn" published
Samuel Ellis advertises to sell Oyster Island (Ellis Island), no takers
Pioneer African Baptist church organizes in Savannah, Georgia
1st US geology book published by William Maclure
Battle of Yungay, Chile defeats a Peruvian and Bolivian alliance
Dumont D'Urville discovers Adélie Land, Antarctica
Dutch King Willem II crowned
China cedes Hong Kong to the British during the First Opium War
Dutch troops conquer Watampone in Celebes
Prim's Insurrection in Spain ends
Florida constitutional convention meets in Tallahassee
"City of Boston" vanishes at sea with all 177 aboard
California Stock Exchange Board organized
British troops under Lord Chelmsford make camp at Isandlwana
Billy Barnes takes a hat-trick, England v Australia at the Melbourne Cricket Ground
US Senate approves naval base lease of Pearl Harbor
Stanley Cup, Dey's Arena, Ottawa, ON: Ottawa Senators outclass Edmonton HC, 13-7 for a 2-0 sweep of challenge series
The first group of Transvaal Indian women satyagrahis are released from Pietermaritzburg Prison in South Africa after three months imprisonment
Dutch 2nd Chamber passes school laws
Russian Imperial Navy Admiral Alexander Kolchak, leader of anti-communist "White Movement" surrenders to Bolshevik troops
The American Civil Liberties Union is founded
British submarine HMS K5 (which was unusually equipped with steam turbines) sinks with 57 crew during exercises in the Bay of Biscay
Dagestan ASSR forms in RSFSR
Mountain Autonomous Republic established in RSFSR
Republic of Turkey is declared from the remnants of the Ottoman Empire
The Soviet and Japan sign a convention resuming relations: Russia agrees to limit revolutionary activity of the Third Communist International, while Japan agrees to leave the Sakhalin
2nd German government of Luther begins
1st feature talking motion picture taken outdoors, "In Old Arizona"
1st radio broadcast of "Lone Ranger" (WXYZ-Detroit)
Japan sends Henry Pu Yi as regent to emperor of Manchuria
-45°F (-43°C), Boca, California (state record)
1st US Presidential Inauguration day held on Jan 20th, (previously March 4th)
Japanese invade Burma
Lead, South Dakota, temperature is 52°F, while 1.5 miles away Deadwood records -16°F
RAF drops 2,300 ton of bombs on Berlin
The Hungarian Provisional Government concludes an armistice with the USSR, US, and Britain, agree to pay reparations and to join the war against Germany
F Gouin follows De Gaulle as temporary leader of French government
Brigadier General Edwin K Wright, USA, becomes deputy director of CIA
Nova Scotia Supreme Court rules against case of black woman Viola Desmond - ejected from a cinema seat in New Glasgow reserved for whites and arrested in landmark case (pardoned 2010)
1st inaugural parade televised, for Harry Truman, watched by 10 million people
1950 NFL Draft: Leon Hart from University of Notre Dame first pick by Detroit Lions
Suriname becomes independent part in Realm of Netherlands
British army occupies Ismailiya, Suez Canal Zone
1st US telecast transmitted to Canada from Buffalo, New York
-70°F (-57°C), Rogers Pass, Montana (US 48 state record)
-70°F measured at Rogers Pass, Montana (record low temperature in the contiguous US)
The National Negro Network is established with 40 charter member radio stations.
Władysław Gomułka wins Poland's parliamentary election
A group attempting the 1st surface crossing of Antarctic join up at the South Pole
KUED TV channel 7 in Salt Lake City, UT (PBS) begins broadcasting
Capitol Records releases "Meet The Beatles" album by the Beatles, in US
The Byrds record "Mr Tambourine Man"
The US Jet Propulsion Laboratory proposes modified Apollo flight to fly around Mars & return
Houston Cougars defeat the UCLA Bruins 71-69 to win basketball's Game of the Century and end UCLA's 47 game winning streak
University of Arizona reports 1st optical identification of a pulsar found in Crab Nebula
Number of people out of work and claiming unemployment benefit in UK rises to over 1 million
Six oil exporting countries conclude meetings with Western oil companies; an agreement is reached to raise the price of crude
A car bomb explodes in Sackville Place, Dublin, Ireland, killing 1 person and injuring 17, no group claims responsibility
Richard Nixon's second inauguration as President of the United States takes place in Washington, D.C. Flags are flown at half-staff in mourning for former President Harry Truman.
Essex Comm College beats Englewood Cliffs 210-67 in basketball
Terrence McNally's play "Ritz" premieres in NYC
George H. W. Bush ends his term as 11th director of CIA replaced by E. Henry Knoche as acting director
Columbia Pictures pays $9.5 million for movie rights to "Annie"
Super Bowl XIV, Rose Bowl, Pasadena, California: Pittsburgh Steelers beat Los Angeles Rams, 31-19; MVP: Terry Bradshaw, Pittsburgh, QB
Adm Stansfield Turner, USN (Ret), ends term as 12th director of CIA
Frank C. Carlucci, ends his term as deputy director of the CIA
Islander Glenn Resch's 25th & last shut-out opponent-Flames 5-0
7 miners killed in an explosion in Craynor Ky
Honduras constitution goes into effect
Piet Dankert elected chairman of European Parliament
Cold front strikes US, at least 40 die (-27°F (-33°C) in Chicago)
Chunnel announced (railroad tunnel under Channel)
Military coup in Lesotho under major general Lekhanya & premier Leabua Jonathan
Anglican Church envoy Terry Waite taken hostage in Beirut, Lebanon by Islamic militia group
Rhino Records 1st #1, Billy Vera & Beaters' "At This Moment"
UK Police crackdown on soccer hooligans in biggest operation against violence around football stadiums
Andre Hoffman skates world record 1,500m (1:52.06)
Arizona committee opens hearing on impeachment of Gov Evan Mecham
Reaganbecomes 1st US President elected in a "0" year, since 1840, to leave office alive
Black January - crackdown of Azerbaijani pro-independence demonstrations by Soviet army in Baku
US 64th manned space mission STS 32 (Columbia 10) returns from space
13th UCP Telethon
Sudan's government imposes Islamic law nationwide, worsening the civil war between the country's Muslim north and Christian south.
US Patriot missiles begins shooting down Iraqi missiles
Score begins selling international soccer cards
Adm Studeman, serves as acting director of CIA
1994-95 NHL Season begin after a lengthy strike
Russian ruble drops to 3,947 per dollar (record)
WPAT FM NYC radio station switches to English-Spanish format
Comet Hale-Bopp crosses Mars' orbit
French electronic dance duo Daft Punk release their album "Homework"
Howard Stern Radio Show premieres in New Orleans, Louisiana on KKND 106.7 FM
Pakistan defeat West Indies 2-0 to win Aust one-day Series
Warner Brothers TV network begins Tueday night programming
The China News Service announces new government restrictions on Internet use aimed especially at Internet cafés.
US PresidentBill Clintonissues 140 executive pardons on his last day in office; recipients include Marc Rich, Susan McDougall, Patty Hearst, and his brother Roger
Photographs showing al-Qaeda and Taliban suspects shackled and masked are published by the United States military
Philip Glass' Symphony No. 7 (Toltec) premieres with the National Symphony Orchestra conducted by Leonard Slatkin, at the Kennedy Center in Washington, D.C.
Frenchman Francis Joyon becomes the fastest person to sail solo around the world, taking 57 days, 13 hours and 34 minutes
Joe Bidenassumes the office of the Vice President of the United States
Austria votes to maintain compulsory military service in a referendum
Credit cards of at least 20 million South Koreans are hacked
Car ploughs into pedestrians in central Melbourne, killing 6 and injuring 27
Center for Disease Control (CDC) reports first laboratory-confirmed case of COVID in the US, from samples taken on January 18 in Washington state
Dan Campbellis appointed head coach of the Detroit Lions

US PresidentJoe Bidensigns 15 executive orders on his first day in office, including: US re-joining the WHO, affirming the Paris Climate Agreement, revoking the Keystone XL Pipeline, mandating masks on federal properties, and halting construction of the southern border wall
19-year-old aviator Zara Rutherford becomes the youngest woman to fly solo around the world landing at Kortrijk-Wevelgem Airport, Belgium
Large weekend protests begin across Germany against far-right Alternative for Germany (AfD), after group's 'master plan' for mass deportation of migrants revealed
Chinese start up DeepSeek launches AI model RI, with capabilities similar to other AI models but at a fraction of the cost, prompting  experts to call it a “Sputnik moment”

In the first day of his return to office, PresidentDonald Trumpissues pardons to over 1500 of his supporters charged in connection with the attack on the US Capitol on January 6, 2021, declares a national emergency at the southern border, and withdraws the US from the World Health Organization and the Paris Agreement on Climate

RepublicanDonald Trumpis inaugurated as the 47th President of the United States of America, and JD Vance as the 50th Vice-President; Trump is oldest person to take the office, and joins Grover Cleveland as only the second elected to non-consecutive White House terms, the event is held indoors due to inclement weather
US PresidentDonald Trumpissues a executive order halting all foreign aid programs for 90 days, with some exceptions for humanitarian aid
US PresidentDonald Trumpissues an executive order for Federal agencies to slash diversity, equity and inclusion initiatives throughout the Federal government
Christian IIsucceeds Johan I as Danish and Norwegian king
John Marshallappointed US chief justice
HMS Investigator headed byRobert McClureleaves England, 1st expedition to make a Northwest passage, although some of the journey was over ice
Elizabeth Cady Stantonbecomes 1st woman to testify before US Congress
Hiram R. Revelselected to fill unexpired term of Albert Brown as US Senator from Mississippi
US begins supervising the Dominican Republic's national and international debts, testing PresidentTheodore Roosevelt's "Corollary" to the Monroe Doctrine
Prime Minister of FranceGeorges Clémenceauresigns and is replaced be Alexandre Millerand
Arthur Honegger's ballet "Skating Rink" premieres at the Théâtre des Champs-Élysées in Paris, France
Edward VIIIsucceeds British King George V
Charles Ives' 1st piano sonata "Concord" premieres
Béla Bartók's 6th String Quartet, premieres in NYC
Chinese Communist leaders reorganize the New Fourth Army in defiance of Nationalist leaderChiang Kai-shek's order for it to be disbanded
Operation Weiss: German, Italian, Bulgarian & Croatian troops attempt to retake land liberated byTito's partisans
Franklin D. Rooseveltsworn-in for an unprecedented (and never to be repeated) 4th term as US President
J. Edgar HoovergivesShirley Templea tear gas fountain pen
US PresidentHarry Trumanannounces his four point program
The NBA's first superstarGeorge Mikanscores a career high 61 points leading the Minneapolis Lakers to a 91-81 double-overtime victory over the Rochester Royals
US PresidentDwight D. Eisenhowerdelivers 1st live US coast-to-coast inauguration address
Dmitri Shostakovich's "Concertino opus 94" premieres

Herman Wouk's stage drama "The Caine Mutiny Court-Martial", adpated from his novel "The Caine Mutiny", starringHenry Fonda, and directed byCharles Laughton, premieres at the Plymouth Theatre, NYC; runs for 415 performances
Buddy Hollyrecords "Blue Days Black Night" in Nashville, Tennessee for country music producer Owen Bradley
Morton Gould's orchestral work "Declaration" premieres in Washington, D.C.
Patrice Lumumbasentenced to 6 months in Belgian Congo jail for inciting an anti-colonial riot

DemocratJohn F. Kennedy, the youngest elected President of the United States, is administered his oath of office at his inauguration by Supreme Court Chief JusticeEarl Warren.Robert Frostrecites "Gift Outright".
Yugoslav ex-vice-presidentMilovan Djilasflees
GeneralissimoFrancisco Francomeets with Jewish representatives to discuss legitimizing Jewish communities in Spain
Philadelphia 76ers centerWilt Chamberlainmakes all 15 of his field goal attempts in a 119-108 win over LA Lakers; NBA record for consecutive shots made; beats mark twice before 1966-67 season ends
Arthur Penn's film "Bonnie and Clyde" premieres in Paris, France with starsWarren Beatty&Faye Dunawayin attendance

Richard Nixoninaugurated as 39th US president, completing his political comeback - he had served as vice president (1953–61), and in congress (1947-53) before losing races for president (1960), and for California governor (1962);  former Maryland governorSpiro Agnewis sworn in as Vice-President
The Super Fight, a computer mock championship boxing bout betweenMuhammad AliandRocky Marciano; Marciano wins with a 13th round KO
Ard Schenkskates world record 1000m (1:18.8)
John LennonmeetsYoko Ono's parents in Japan
Tamla Records releasesMarvin Gaye's single "What's Going On" about police brutality, from the album of the same name
1 million Iranians march in Tehran in a show of support for the exiledAyatollah Khomeini, fundamentalist Muslim leader
US PresidentJimmy Carterannounces US boycott of winter Olympics in Moscow after the Soviet invasion of Afghanistan
Ronald Reaganinaugurated as the 40th President of the United States of America
Future Hockey Hall of Fame centerDarryl Sittleris traded by the Toronto Maple Leafs to the Philadelphia Flyers in exchange for Rich Costello, a 2nd round draft pick and future considerations
Heavy metal musicianOzzy Osbourneallegedly bites the head off a bat on stage in Des Moines, Iowa
American gangsterRoy DeMeois found murdered in his car trunk after disappearing a few days earlier
Super Bowl XIX, Stanford Stadium, Stanford, CA: San Francisco 49ers beat Miami Dolphins, 38-16; MVP:Joe Montana, SF, QB
1st US federal holiday honoringMartin Luther King Jr.or MLK Day
3rd Rock and Roll Hall of Fame inductees: The Beach Boys; The Beatles; The Drifters;Bob Dylan;The Supremes;Woody Guthrie; Leadbelly; Les Paul; andBerry Gordy, Jr.
George H. W. Bushinaugurated as 41st US President, Dan Quayle becomes 44th Vice President
Pittsburgh centerMario Lemieuxbecomes just the 2nd player in NHL history to score 50 goals in less than 50 games; #50 in 44th game as the Penguins lose 7-3 to the Jets in Winnipeg
Bill Clinton(D) inaugurated as 42nd US President
Maya Angelouis 1st African American and woman to recite a poem at inauguration of a US President - "On the Pulse of Morning" forBill Clinton
"Dawson's Creek" created by Kevin Williamson, starringJames Van Der Beek,Katie Holmesand Joshua Jackson premieres on WB in the US
George W. Bushinaugurated as 43rd US President,Dick Cheneybecomes 46th Vice President
President of the PhilippinesJoseph Estradais ousted in the EDSA II Revolution and succeeded byGloria Macapagal-Arroyo.
"High School Musical" debuts, starring Zac Efron and Vanessa Hugdens; becomes Disney Channel's most successful TV movie
Utah coachJerry Sloanpasses Larry Brown for 4th on the NBA's all-time win list (1,010) after the Jazz beat the Chicago Bulls, 95-85 at the United Center
"Breaking Bad", created by Vince Gilligan and starringBryan CranstonandAaron Paulpremieres on AMC
Barack Obamais inaugurated as the 44th President of the United States of America, becoming the United States' first African-American president
Musical "Hamilton" starringLin-Manuel MirandaandAnthony Ramospremieres at The Public Theater in Lower Manhattan, New York (Pulitzer Prize for Drama 2016)
Donald Trumpis inaugurated as the 45th President of the United States of America andMike Penceas the 48th Vice President

Joe Bidenis inaugurated as the 46th President of the United States of America andKamala Harrisas the 49th Vice President, the first female, black or south Asian Vice President.Amanda Gormanrecites "The Hill We Climb"; previous office holderDonald Trumpis first outgoing president to refuse to attend the inauguration of his successor since 1869 P
Marco Rubiois confirmed as US Secretary of State by the US Senate
Nazi officials hold the notorious Wannsee Conference in Berlin to organize the "final solution", the extermination of Europe's Jews
The US diplomats and citizens held hostage at the US embassy in Tehran are released and begin their journey home after 444 days
Gordian III, Roman Emperor (238-44) who became the youngest Roman Emperor at 13 years, born in Rome (d. 244)
Alp Arslan, Turkish sultan of the Seljuk Empire (1063-72), born in Persia (d. 1072)
Elisabeth I of Bohemia, Queen of Bohemia, daughter of king Wenceslaus II, born in Prague (d. 1330)
Ashikaga Yoshimasa, 8th Japanese shōgun of the Ashikaga shogunate (1443-73), born in Japan (d. 1490)
Sebastian, King of Portugal (1557-78), born in Ribeira Palace, Lisbon, Kingdom of Portugal (d. 1578)
Johann Hermann Schein, German composer (Fontana d'Israel), born in Grünhain, Saxony (d. 1630)
Susanna van Baerle, Dutch poet (wife of Geeraardt Brandt), born in Leiden, Netherlands (d. 1674)
Edward Tyson, English physician and father of comparative anatomy (The Anatomy of a Pygmy), born in Somerset, England (d. 1708)
Michiel de Swaen, Dutch physician and poet, born in Dunkirk (d. 1701)
Giovanni Vincenzo Gravina, Italian scholar and  jurist known for "Originum juris civilis", born in Roggiano Gravina, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1718)
Francesco Bartolomeo Conti, Italian composer and mandolin player, born in Florence, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (d. 1732)
Joseph-Hector Fiocco, Flemish composer, born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1741)
Carlos III, King of Spain (1759-88) and King of Naples and Sicily (1734-59), born in Royal Alcazar of Madrid, Spain (d. 1788)
Jean-Jacques Barthélemy, French archaeologist and novelist known for "Voyage du jeune Anacharsis en Grèce", born in Cassis, France (d. 1795)
Pascal Boyer, French composer, born in Tarascon, Provence, France (d. 1794)
Marie-Anne Paulze Lavoisier, French chemist (worked with husband Antoine Lavoisier), born in Montbrison, France (d. 1836)
Giovanni Domenico Perotti, Italian composer, born in Vercelli, Kingdom of Sardinia (d. 1855)
Jerome-Joseph de Momigny, Belgian French composer, born in Philippeville, Belgium (d. 1842)
André-Marie Ampère, French physicist who pioneered electromagnetism, unit of current the ampere named after him, born in Lyon, France  (d. 1836)
Joseph Hormayr Freiherr zu Hortenburg, Austrian politician, born in Innsbruck, Austria (d. 1848)
Archduke John of Austria, Austrian general and Regent of Germany (1848), born in Florence, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (now Italy) (d. 1859)
Friedrich Dotzauer, German cellist (Dresden Court Orchestra, 1821-50), composer, and teacher, born in Haselrieth, Duchy of Saxe-Hildburghausen (d. 1860)
Anson Jones, 5th and last President of Texas, born in Great Barrington, Massachusetts (d. 1858)
Eugène Sue, French writer of serial novels, born in Paris (d. 1857)
Nathaniel Parker Willis, American writer, editor and founder of the American Monthly Magazine, born in Portland, Maine (d. 1867)
Sebastian de Iradier, Spanish-Basque composer (La Paloma), born in Lanciego, Álava, Spain (d. 1865)
Ralph Pomeroy Buckland, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Leyden, Massachusetts (d. 1892)
Thomas Meik, Scottish civil engineer who built bridges, ports and railways (Harlow Group), born in Duddingston, Scotland (d. 1896)
Jacon Gartner Lauman, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Taneytown, Maryland (d. 1867)
Anne Clough, English suffragette, promoted higher education for women, first principal of New College, born in Liverpool, England (d. 1892)
Henry G. Blasdel, American politician (1st Governor of Nevada, 1864-71), and miner, born in Cambridge, Indiana (d. 1900)
Piet Joubert, South African general and vice-president, born in Prince Albert, British Cape Colony (d. 1900)
George D. Robinson, 34th Governor of Massachusetts (1884-87) and lawyer who defended Lizzie Borden, born in Lexington, Massachusetts (d. 1896)
Pierre-Paul Cambon, French diplomat (Madrid, Constantinople, London), born in Paris, France (d. 1924)
Johan Peter Selmer, Norwegian composer, born in Christiania, Norway (d. 1910)
William R. Pettiford, African American minister and banker, founder of the Alabama Penny Savings Bank, born in Granville county, North Carolina (d. 1914)
Ernest Chausson, French Romantic composer (Poème; Solitude dance le bois), born in Paris, France (d. 1899)
Friedrich, German prince, last ruler of Waldeck and Pyrmont, born in Arolsen, Waldeck and Pyrmont (d. 1946)
Richard Le Gallienne, English writer (had affair with Oscar Wilde, father of actress Eva Le Gallienne), born in Liverpool, England (d. 1947)
Yvette Guilbert [Emma Laure Esther Guilbert], French singer and actress, born in Paris, France (d. 1944)
Guillaume Lekeu, Belgian composer (Épithalame), born in Heusy, Belguim (d. 1894)

Julia Morgan, American architect (Hearst Castle, and other Hearst properties), first female degreed by Ecole des Beaux-Arts, and first licensed in California, born in San Francisco, California (d. 1957)[1]
Charles A. Ellwood, American sociologist and psychologist, born near Ogdensburg, New York (d. 1946)
Johannes V. Jensen, Danish novelist and poet (The Fall of the King; Nobel Prize for Literature, 1944), born in Farsø, North Jutland, Denmark (d. 1950)
Józef Hofmann, Polish-American pianist, composer, educator (Curtis Institute, 1924-38), and inventor, born in Podgórze, Austro-Hungarian Galicia (now Poland) (d. 1957)
William Veeck, Sr, American sportswriter and baseball executive (Chicago Cubs president, 1919-33), born in  Boonville, Indiana (d. 1933)[1]
Ruth St. Denis, American ballerina (Dances of the 5 Senses), born in Newark, New Jersey (d. 1968)
Finlay Currie, Scottish actor (Quo Vadis, Ivanhoe, Avengers), born in Edinburgh (d. 1968)
Walter W. Bacon, American politician, Governor of Delaware (1941-49), born in New Castle, Delaware (d. 1962)
Bertram Home Ramsay, English naval officer (Admiral who oversaw Dunkirk evacuations and Normandy landings), born in London, England (d. 1945)
(Abraham) A. Merritt, American magazine editor and sci-fi author (The Moon Pool; Burn, Witch, Burn!), born in Beverly, New Jersey (d. 1943)
Allan Lockheed [Allan Haines Loughead], American aviator engineer and executive (Lockheed Corporation), born in Niles, California (d. 1969)
Mischa Elman, Russian-American concert violinist, born in Talnoye, Kiev Governorate, Russian Empire (now Talne, Ukraine (d. 1967)
Georg Åberg, Swedish athlete (Olympic silver triple jump; bronze long jump 1912), born in Norrköping, Sweden (d. 1946)
Kaj Birket-Smith, Danish etnologist and anthropologist (Inuit and Eyak), born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1977)
Harold Gray, American comic strip artist who created "Little Orphan Annie" (1924), born in Kankakee, Illinois (d. 1968)
Eva Jessye, American singer, actress, songwriter (Hallelujah) and 1st black woman to receive international distinction as a choral conductor, born in Coffeyville, Kansas (d. 1992)
Roscoe Ates, American actor (Freaks, Cimarron, Marshal of Gunsight Pass), born in Grange, Mississippi (d. 1962)
Elmer Diktonius, Finnish poet, composer, and musicologist (Janne Kubrik; Stenkol), born in Helsinki, Finland (d. 1961)
George Burns [Nathan Birnbaum], American actor, comedian and singer (Oh God; Burns and Allen), born in New York City (d. 1996)
Isabel Withers, American actress (Possessed, Lady of Burlesque), born in Frankton, Indiana (d. 1968)
Rolfe Sedan, American actor (Young Frankenstein, Ninotchka, George Burns Show), born in New York City (d. 1982)
U Razak, Burmese politician, one of 19 independence leaders assassinated on Martyr's Day, born in Meiktila, British Burma, (d. 1947)
Kenjiro Takayanagi, Japanese engineer, born in Hamamatsu, Shizuoka (d. 1990)
Boris Semyonovich Shekhter, Russian composer, born in Moscow, Russia (d. 1961)
Colin Clive, British actor (Frankenstein, Bride of Frankenstein, Mad Love), born in St Malo, France (d. 1937)
Doris Deane [Dibble], American silent film actress (Stupid, But Brave), born in Wisconsin (d. 1974)
Paul Andor [Wolfgang Zilzer], American-German actor (Mad Lover; Enemy of Women), born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1991)
Leon Ames, American actor (Mister Ed, Father of the Bride), born in Portland, Indiana (d. 1993)
Sybil Marion Rosenfeld, English theatre historian, born in London, England (d. 1996)
Renato Caccioppoli, Italian mathematician, born in Campania, Italy (d. 1959)
Theodore Brameld, American philosopher and educator (Use of Explosive Ideas), born in Neillsville, Wisconsin (d. 1987)
Aristotle Onassis, Greek-Argentine business magnate, owner of world's largest shipping fleet, and second husband of Jacqueline Kennedy, born in Konak, Turkey (d. 1975)
Paula Wessely, Austrian actress and producer (Masquerade), born in Vienna, Austria-Hungary (d. 2000)
Roy Welensky, Northern Rhodesian politician, born in Harare, Zimbabwe (d. 1991)
Ian Peebles, English cricket spin bowler (13 Tests, 45 wickets, BB 6/63; Oxford University CC, Middlesex CCC), born in Aberdeen, Scotland (d. 1980)
Wilfred Conwell Bain, American music educator (Insian University, 1947-73), born in Shawville, Quebec (d. 1997)
Abram Hill, African-American playwright and author (American Negro Theater), born in Atlanta, Georgia (d. 1986)
Ennio Porrino, Italian composer, born in Cagliari, Italy (d. 1959)
Lauritz Lauritzen, German politician, born in Kiel, Germany (d. 1980)
Nina Verchinina, Russian-Brazilian ballet dancer and choreographer, born in Moscow, Russia (d. 1995)[1]
Christopher Casson, Irish-English actor (Zardoz, Educating Rita, The Riordans), born in Manchester, Lancashire, England (d. 1996)
Walter Briggs Jr, American sports executive (owner Detroit Tigers 1952-56), born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 1970)
Gerald Holtom, British artist and graphic designer (Nuclear Disarmament (ND) logo, which later became an international peace symbol), born in Twickenham, England (d. 1985)
Wensley Pithey, South-African actor (Oliver!, The Saint, Little Women), born in Cape Town, South Africa (d. 1993)
C. W. Ceram, German/ journalist and author (March of Archaeology), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1972)
Ghulam Ishaq Khan, President of Pakistan, born in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan (d. 2006)
Alex Nicol, American actor (The Screaming Skull, Man From Laramie, Air Cadet), born in Ossining, New York (d. 2001)
Johan Adolf Pengel, Surinamese politician (Prime Minister, 1963-69), born in Paramaribo, Suriname (d. 1970)
Walter Bartley, English biochemist, born in Brighton, London (d. 1994)
Esquivel! [Juan García Esquivel], Mexican pianist, composer, and bandleader known as the "King of Space-Age Pop", born in Tampico, Mexico (d. 2002)
Royalton Kisch, British conductor, born in Marylebone, London, England (d. 1995)
Stepan Lucky, Slovak film score composer, born in Žilina, Slovakia (d. 2006)
DeForest Kelley, American actor (Dr. Leonard "Bones" McCoy-Star Trek), born in Atlanta, Georgia (d. 1999)
Ely Landau, American television and film producer (Play of the Week; Long Day's Journey into Night; King: A Filmed Record... Montgomery to Memphis), born in New York City (d. 1993)
Michael Higgins, American actor (Angel Heart, 1918, Wanda, Black Stallion), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2008)
Peter Clemoes, British historian, born in Southend-on-Sea, United Kingdom (d. 1996)
Richard J. C. Atkinson, British archaeologist, born in Evershot, United Kingdom (d. 1994)
Sam Gibbons, American politician (Rep-D-FL, 1963-97), born in Tampa, Florida (d. 2012)
Thorleif Schjelderup, Norwegian author and ski jumper (Olympic bronze Individual large hill 1948), born in Aker, Norway (d. 2006)
Bernt Engelmann, German writer, born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1994)
Connie Haines [Yvonne Jasme], American pop and big-band singer (Harry James; Abbott and Costello Show; Tommy Dorsey), born in Savannah, Georgia (d. 2008)[1]
Telmo Zarraonaindía, Spanish soccer forward (20 caps, 20 goals; Athletic Bilbao 277 games, 251 goals), born in Erandio, Spain (d. 2006)
James Hanson, English industrialist, born in Huddersfield, United Kingdom (d. 2004)
Ray Anthony [Antonini], American orchestra leader (Ray Anthony Show; "Peter Gunn Theme"), born in Bentleyville, Pennsylvania
(Ottis) "Slim" Whitman, American country singer ("Rose Marie"; "Guess Who"), born in Tampa, Florida (d. 2013)
Nora Brockstedt, Norwegian pop and jazz singer, born in Oslo, Norway (d. 2015)
Yvonne Loriod, French pianist, composer (Mélopées africaines), and teacher, born in Houilles, France (d. 2010)
Ernesto Cardenal, Nicaraguan Catholic priest, politician, poet, and writer, born in Granada, Nicaragua (d. 2020)
Eugen Gomringer, Bolivian-born German poet, born in Cachuela Esperanza, Bolivia
David Tudor, American composer, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1996)
Jamiluddin Aali, Pakistani poet and writer, born in New Delhi, India (d. 2015)
